metaTitle: Critical Mistakes to Avoid Before Picking an Attorney
metaDescription: Most accident victims make at least one of these critical mistakes before speaking to an attorney. Learn what to avoid to protect your personal injury claim.
label: Critical
headline: Do Not Do This Before Picking an Attorney
teaser: Most accident victims make at least one of these mistakes before they ever speak to an attorney. Each one seems harmless — but can reduce or eliminate your claim.
teaserBullets: Talking to the other driver's insurance adjuster alone
Signing any document from the other party's insurer
Waiting more than 48 hours to see a doctor
Posting about the accident on social media

content.heading: Don't give a recorded statement to the other driver's insurance company
content.body: The adjuster's job is to minimize your payout — not help you. A recorded statement gives them ammunition to use against you. Politely decline and let them know your attorney will be in touch. You are not legally required to provide one.

content.heading: Don't sign any release or settlement offer without legal review
content.body: Early settlement offers are almost always lowball figures designed to close your claim before the full extent of your injuries is known. Once you sign a release, you cannot reopen the claim — even if you discover more serious injuries months later.

content.heading: Don't delay medical treatment
content.body: Insurance companies use gaps in treatment as evidence that your injuries weren't serious. See a doctor within 48–72 hours, even if you feel okay. Adrenaline masks pain — symptoms often worsen in the days following an accident.

content.heading: Don't post about your accident on social media
content.body: Photos, check-ins, activity updates — even vague posts — can be used against you. Defense attorneys routinely monitor claimants' social media. A single photo of you at a social event could be used to argue your injuries aren't as severe as claimed.

content.heading: Don't wait too long to consult an attorney
content.body: California and Arizona both have a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims. Evidence disappears, witnesses forget, and your negotiating leverage shrinks every day you wait. A free consultation costs you nothing.
